issues.where: The whole-memo jargon load: "Lode universal-capture remainder", "the divine/augur read verbs", "the per-kind capture bodies", "the cutover chokepoints", "bole capture", "enshrine path", "rbi_es/<fp> vs rbi_ld/<stamp>:<fp>", "touchmark", "reliquary", "podvm", "fetter, conclave, underpin, immure".
issues.problem: The reader asked to be EDUCATED ABOUT A PROCESS (the multi-agent workflow). But the memo teaches the process while assuming fluency in the Lode domain it was applied to. A smart reader who knows the ultracode mechanics but not this codebase's vocabulary will stall repeatedly on domain nouns that are used but never explained. The teaching target (the process) gets blurred by an un-taught second subject (the domain). The reader cannot tell which words they are supposed to learn here and which they are assumed to already own.
issues.fix: Add one or two sentences early (right after the opening, before "The shape of the run") that say explicitly: "You don't need to know the Lode domain to follow this memo — wherever a domain word like 'bole' or 'reliquary' appears, read it as 'one of the subsystems being investigated.' What matters is the process around it." Then, at the few spots where a domain detail is actually load-bearing for the teaching point (the rbi_es/rbi_ld example), add a half-sentence gloss: "...the old path stored the image at one address and the new Lode path at a differently-shaped one, so the new address can't be reconstructed from the inputs the way the old one could."

issues.where: Section "The shape of the run": "The four open calls each touched a *different* part of the codebase" — but the diagram and the surrounding text list FIVE investigators (augur, scaffold, vertical, cutover, provdocs), and "Ten agents total: five to find the facts..."
issues.problem: Four-vs-five is a genuine comprehension snag for the careful reader the memo is written for. The memo says four open calls, then runs five investigators, and never explains why the count changed. A teaching memo must not leave the reader silently doing arithmetic that doesn't add up. (This is a clarity gap, not a fact error — the fact may be perfectly correct; it is simply unexplained.)
issues.fix: Add one sentence where the five investigators first appear: "(The four calls became five readers because one call — provenance and docs — spanned two surfaces wide enough to warrant its own agent each; the mapping from questions to agents is not always 1:1.)" Or whatever the real reason is. The point is to name the 4→5 jump out loud the moment it occurs.

issues.where: Section "What 'ultracode' changed": "The lever is the **Workflow tool**: a small JavaScript program that spawns subagents..." and later "author a workflow that decomposes the task across many subagents."
issues.problem: This is the one piece of genuinely new machinery the reader is being educated about, and it is introduced too fast to picture. "A small JavaScript program that spawns subagents" raises immediate questions the memo never answers: who writes the program (the answer — Claude writes it on the fly — is buried in the verb "author")? Is it run once, or is it a standing tool? What does a subagent actually receive and return? Without a concrete mental image of one subagent call, the rest of the architecture is abstract.
issues.fix: Add two or three sentences making one subagent call concrete: "Concretely: I write a short script. It loops, and on each pass it launches a fresh Claude instance — a subagent — handing it a focused prompt ("read these files, answer this one question, return your findings in this exact shape"). That subagent has its own clean context, does its reading, returns a structured result, and disappears. The script collects those results for me. I author this script per-task; it is not a fixed pipeline." This grounds every later mention of "agent" in something the reader can see.

issues.where: Design choice 4: "**Pipeline, don't barrier.** The five areas have no dependency on each other, so there was no reason to wait for all five investigations to finish before starting any verification... The whole job finished at the speed of the slowest single chain, not the sum of the stages."
issues.problem: "Barrier" is used as the contrast term but never defined — it is concurrency jargon (a barrier = a sync point where all parallel work must finish before anyone proceeds). The reader is asked to understand the WHY of pipeline-not-barrier without being told what a barrier is. The payoff sentence ("speed of the slowest single chain, not the sum of the stages") is correct but compressed; a busy reader has to stop and verify the timing claim themselves.
issues.fix: Define the contrast in plain words: "The obvious way (a 'barrier') is two phases: run all five investigations, wait for the last one, THEN start verifying. That wastes time — verification of the area that finished first sits idle waiting for the slowest investigation. Instead, each area's skeptic starts the instant that area's investigation lands. The areas run as five independent investigate-then-verify chains, side by side, so total time is the longest single chain rather than (all investigations) + (all verifications)."

issues.where: Design choice 5 and the false-positive section both carry the human-judgment point: "machines gather, human-in-the-loop judges" and "The human in the loop exists precisely to reconcile agent output against knowledge the agents didn't have."
issues.problem: These are the two strongest teaching moments in the memo and they make essentially the same point in two places, ~12 lines apart, without the second acknowledging the first. The reader gets the abstract version (design choice 5) and then the concrete version (the paddock false-positive) as if they were unrelated. The memo doesn't connect them, so the concrete story doesn't land as the proof of the earlier principle — it reads as a second, separate observation. The teaching would be far stronger if the abstract claim were stated once and then explicitly cashed out by the story.
issues.fix: Make the false-positive story the payoff of design choice 5 rather than a free-floating lesson. In the verify section, open the false-positive paragraph with an explicit callback: "This is design choice 5 ("keep the judgment") earning its keep — here is exactly the kind of thing it exists to catch:" Then keep the story. That turns two restatements into one principle-plus-evidence, which is how the reader will actually remember it.

issues.where: Design choice 2: "a single *recommendation input*" and design choice 1: "the divine/augur read verbs, the per-kind capture bodies, the cutover chokepoints, the provenance/docs surface."
issues.problem: "Recommendation input" is an undefined coinage at the moment of use — the reader can guess it means "the agent's suggested takeaway for the final decision," but a teaching memo shouldn't make the reader guess a term it just invented. Separately, the parenthetical list in choice 1 is the densest domain-jargon clause in the memo and arrives right where the reader is trying to grasp the general principle ("fan out by question, not by file"), competing with it for attention.
issues.fix: Gloss the coinage inline: "a single *recommendation input* — the agent's own one-line suggestion for what the final call should be." And in choice 1, demote the jargon list to an aside or replace it with the count: "The four calls map to disjoint subsystems — read verbs, capture bodies, cutover points, provenance/docs. One agent holding all four would read shallowly across everything; four (here, five) agents each read one area deeply." Let the principle breathe without the full domain vocabulary riding on top of it.

issues.where: Overall length and the final two sections ("When this is the right tool..." and "A closing note").
issues.problem: The memo's teaching core is the design-choices section and the verify-caught section — those are tight and earn their length. The tail sags slightly: "When this is the right tool" is good and belongs, but "A closing note" (the memo was itself reviewed by ultracode) is a cute reflexive flourish that adds no teaching and competes with the genuinely useful self-aware point already made at the end of the previous section ("this very memo was a borderline case"). The reader's attention is finite; the last thing they read should be the durable takeaway, not a meta-curio.
issues.fix: Either cut "A closing note" entirely, or fold its one substantive fact (independent agents checked this memo) into a single clause on the borderline-case sentence in the prior section: "...so the subagents could only review a draft I wrote — which is in fact how this memo was checked — rather than author it from scratch." End the memo on the when-to-use-it judgment, which is the highest-value parting thought.

result.verdict: As a teaching piece the memo is genuinely good — the five-design-choices spine is the right structure, and the verify-caught and false-positive sections are vivid and memorable. It is close to shippable but not yet there for its stated reader. The single most important change: the memo teaches the PROCESS while silently assuming fluency in the LODE DOMAIN it was run on, so a reader who knows multi-agent workflows but not this codebase stalls on un-taught nouns (bole, reliquary, touchmark, augur/divine, the rbi_es/rbi_ld example). Add one early sentence telling the reader they may read every domain word as \"one of the subsystems under investigation,\" and gloss the two or three spots where a domain detail is actually load-bearing. Fix the unexplained 4-calls-but-5-investigators jump in the same pass. Those two changes remove the only places a smart, busy reader actually stumbles; everything else is polish.

issues.where: Section 'What the verify stage actually caught' — 'And one false positive worth dwelling on. A skeptic flagged the per-kind verb names (fetter, conclave, underpin, immure) as fabricated — invented by the investigator. They were not: they are the paddock's settled vocabulary. ... I caught this because I did have the paddock'
issues.problem: This is the memo's most flattering self-narrative and it misrepresents what actually happened. The verbs fetter/conclave/underpin/immure were NOT invented by the investigator — they were injected by the workflow's own SHARED prompt (script lines 56-57: 'Remaining kinds to build as verticals: tool (verb fetter, rbw-lF), reliquary (conclave, rbw-lC)...'). The investigator merely echoed the prompt's vocabulary. The skeptic's correction (results, provdocs/vertical corrections item 1) was factually CORRECT about the code: these verbs 'exist nowhere in Tools/rbk.' So the skeptic was not a false positive in the code-vs-code sense at all — it correctly reported that the verbs are not in the codebase. The memo recasts a correct code-finding as a skeptic error, then credits the human (and 'the paddock') with the save. The claim that the verbs are 'the paddock's settled vocabulary' is asserted with zero evidence in either artifact, and the verbs demonstrably originated in the script author's prompt, not in a design doc the agents couldn't see. The morally-tidy 'verifier is only as good as its context' lesson is built on a misdescription of the mechanism.
issues.fix: Rewrite this honestly: the per-kind verbs were seeded by the workflow prompt itself (cite script lines 56-57), so the investigators inherited them rather than fabricating them. State plainly that the skeptics were CORRECT that the verbs are absent from code, and that whether they are settled vocabulary is a separate, paddock-level question the agents had no access to. If the verbs really are paddock-settled, cite the paddock. Otherwise, recast this as 'the workflow prompt over-asserted vocabulary as settled, and the verify stage correctly caught that it isn't grounded in code' — which is a real weakness in how the run was authored, not a verifier failure.

issues.where: Section 'What it cost' — 'Ten agents, ~937,000 subagent tokens, ~200 tool calls, about eight minutes of wall-clock.'
issues.problem: None of these three numbers is corroborated by the artifacts provided. The results file (wl3aagbey.output) reports agentCount: 10 and logs: [] but contains NO token count, NO tool-call count, and NO wall-clock time. The script likewise emits none of these. The memo presents precise-looking figures (~937,000 tokens, ~200 calls, ~8 min) as the central evidence for the cost/benefit trade, yet a reader cannot reproduce or verify any of them from what is cited. A memo whose explicit purpose is to 'educate the operator on the process' is leaning its entire cost argument on unsourced numbers.
issues.fix: Either cite where these figures came from (a harness usage report, a billing readout, a log file path) and add that path to the memo's evidence trail, or mark them explicitly as approximate operator-observed values. At minimum say 'per the harness usage summary (not in the results file)' so the reader knows the numbers live outside the two artifacts the memo otherwise grounds itself in.

issues.where: Overall framing — the memo names exactly three corrections plus 'one false positive' and concludes the verify stage 'earned their place,' but never reports the verify stage's actual hit-rate or its limits as a check.
issues.problem: The results show the verify stage's net effect was thinner than the memo implies. Across all five areas, EVERY recommendation 'STANDS' per the skeptics — not one recommendation was reversed. Only ONE of ~40 verdicts came back holds:false (vertical claim 7, trust grades). The rest of the 'corrections' are citation line-number slips and prose nuances explicitly labeled 'do NOT change the recommendation.' So the honest summary is: the verify stage changed zero conclusions and corrected one over-confident fact plus a handful of stale citations. The memo's 'single most valuable part of the run' framing oversells a stage that, on this run, validated rather than altered the plan. That is still useful (confidence), but the memo should not let the reader infer the skeptics rescued the plan from error.
issues.fix: Add one honest sentence of scorecard: 'Across five areas the skeptics reversed zero recommendations and flipped exactly one over-confident fact (the trust-grade claim); the remaining corrections were citation slips and framing nuances. The verify stage bought confidence and caught one real over-claim — it did not change the plan.' This makes the cost/benefit trade legible instead of implied.

issues.where: Section 'The shape of the run' / diagram and design choice 2 — the memo describes the structured schema and 'confidence level' but never explains what confidence levels did or why they mattered, and omits that the investigators' self-assigned confidence was itself unreliable.
issues.problem: The prompt asked the user to ensure the role of confidence levels is explained. The memo mentions confidence only once (design choice 2) as part of the required shape, and never says what it bought. Worse, the run's own data undercuts confidence as a signal: the trust-grade claim that the skeptic falsified was marked 'high confidence' by the investigator (results vertical claim 7), and the wsl-fetch-mechanism inference was also high-confidence-but-unsupported. So the single most important fact about confidence levels on this run is that high-confidence claims were among the wrong ones — i.e., self-reported confidence did NOT correlate with correctness, which is exactly why the independent skeptic was needed. The memo omits this entirely.
issues.fix: Add a short paragraph: confidence levels let the human triage which claims to scrutinize, but this run showed self-reported confidence is not trustworthy on its own — the two facts the skeptics had to correct/flag (trust grades, wsl fetch mechanism) were both stamped 'high.' That is the real argument for adversarial verification: you cannot rely on an agent's own confidence, so a second agent must re-derive it. This both explains the role of confidence AND strengthens the verify-stage rationale with evidence.

issues.where: Section 'Keep the judgment' / design choice 5 — 'The agents produced facts; I produced the recommendation.'
issues.problem: This clean machines-gather/human-judges split is overstated. The investigation schema's recommendation_input field (script lines 26, 74, 87, 100, 113, 124) required each investigator to produce a recommendation — 'classify augur as (a)/(b)/(c)/(d),' 'group the easy ones, or split podvm,' 'slate bole-cutover EARLY.' The agents did not just produce facts; they produced the very recommendations ('group tool+reliquary, split podvm, slate the bole cutover early') that the memo (line 40) attributes to the human as 'mine.' The skeptics then ratified those recommendations. The human's contribution was real (selection, the paddock override) but the memo erases how much of the recommendation shape was authored by the subagents and merely adopted.
issues.fix: State it accurately: the agents produced both facts AND candidate recommendations (the recommendation_input field), the skeptics stress-tested those recommendations, and the human selected/adjudicated among them — including one override the agents could not make (the paddock vocabulary). 'Machines gather, human judges' is too clean; 'machines gather and propose, human adjudicates and reconciles against private context' is what the script shows.

issues.where: Section 'What it cost' and 'When this is the right tool' — the memo lists when ultracode fits but understates the structural limitation that the subagents had no access to the paddock/design context.
issues.problem: The single most consequential limit of this run is buried as a one-line lesson at the end of the verify section: the subagents never received the paddock, so they could only check code-against-code. This is not a minor footnote — it determined what the whole run could and could not conclude. Every 'recommendation' the agents made about pace boundaries (one-per-kind vs group, slate-early vs paired) is a planning judgment that genuinely needs the design context the agents lacked, yet the workflow asked them for exactly those judgments. The memo's 'When this is the right tool' section never names 'the relevant knowledge is split between code (agents have it) and design docs (agents don't)' as a precondition that was only half-met here.
issues.fix: Promote the context-gap from a closing lesson to a stated limit of the approach. Add to 'When this is the right tool': this shape works when the answer lives in the code the agents can read; it degrades when the decision also depends on private design context (the paddock) the agents were not given — which was true here, and is why the human override was load-bearing rather than optional. Consider noting the obvious mitigation the run did NOT take: feeding the paddock into the agents' context.

issues.where: Section 'How synthesis actually worked' is missing — 'I then read all ten results and wrote the recommendations myself' (line 28) is the only description.
issues.problem: The prompt explicitly asks how synthesis actually worked, and the memo does not answer it. It says the human 'read all ten results and wrote the recommendations,' but gives no account of the actual synthesis mechanics: there was no synthesis agent or merge stage in the workflow — the script's pipeline returns the raw 10-element results array (script line 153 'return results') with zero aggregation. So synthesis happened entirely outside the workflow, in the human/orchestrator's head, against the four original planning calls. A reader trying to reproduce this would not learn that the workflow itself does no synthesis — it is purely investigate+verify, and the join from 'four planning calls' to 'ten-pace slate' is undocumented.
issues.fix: Add a short 'How the findings became a plan' paragraph: the workflow performed no synthesis — it returned ten raw investigate+verify results (script returns the array unmerged). Synthesis was a separate manual step where the orchestrator mapped each area's verified recommendation_input back onto the four original calls and onto the 10-pace slate. Name where the four-calls-to-ten-paces mapping lives, so the reader can see the actual decision artifact, not just 'I wrote it myself.'

issues.where: Section 'The shape of the run' — 'The four open calls each touched a different part of the codebase, so the natural move was to give each its own reader. The workflow ran five investigators...'
issues.problem: Four calls, five investigators — the memo never reconciles the mismatch. The script shows the fifth area ('provdocs') actually bundles TWO distinct questions (provenance attachment AND public docs, script lines 118-124), and the four calls were re-cut into five areas (augur, scaffold, vertical, cutover, provdocs) that do not map 1:1 onto the operator's four planning calls. A reader trying to understand the decomposition will be confused by 'four calls → five readers' with no explanation, and by the implicit claim of disjointness when provdocs is itself two-in-one.
issues.fix: Explain the re-cut: the four operator calls were decomposed into five investigation areas (one call split, or a cross-cutting concern carved out), and note that provdocs deliberately carries two sub-questions. A one-line mapping table (call → area) would make the 'fan out by question' design choice actually inspectable.

issues.where: Section 'Force structured answers' — 'it lets me combine ten results mechanically instead of parsing ten free-text essays' and the broader omission of why structured schemas enable retries.
issues.problem: The prompt specifically asks the memo to convey 'why structured schemas allow model retries.' The memo gives the legibility/mechanical-combination benefit but never mentions the retry/validation benefit at all: a JSON-schema-constrained agent output can be machine-validated and the agent re-invoked on malformed output, which is a core operational reason these workflows use schemas. The memo also slightly overstates 'combine mechanically' — the results were NOT mechanically combined; they were read and synthesized by a human (see synthesis gap above).
issues.fix: Add the retry rationale: the fixed schema (script INVESTIGATION_SCHEMA / VERIFICATION_SCHEMA) lets the harness validate each agent's output shape and re-run an agent that returns malformed or incomplete structure, which is what makes a 10-agent fan-out reliable rather than fragile. And soften 'combine mechanically' to 'collate' since the actual combination was human judgment over a uniform shape, not an automated merge.

issues.where: Closing note — 'This memo was itself put through a small ultracode review panel ... before being committed. The process described here was used to refine the description of itself.'
issues.problem: This is a self-congratulatory flourish that, on inspection, slightly oversells. The memo earlier admits (line 62) this memo was a 'borderline case' where subagents 'could only review a draft I wrote — they couldn't have authored it from scratch.' The closing note recasts that limitation as a virtue ('the process refined the description of itself') without re-stating the limit. A skeptical reader sees the same self-referential review being framed once as a caveat and once as a triumph.
issues.fix: Make the two mentions consistent: the review panel checked a human-authored draft for accuracy against the script/results (a genuine but bounded use), and could not have produced the memo. Drop or qualify 'the process was used to refine the description of itself' so it does not read as a stronger claim than the borderline-case admission ten lines earlier.

result.verdict: Do not ship as written. The memo is clear and well-organized, and three of its four 'what verify caught' examples (trust grades, byte-equivalent coordinate, citation slips) are accurately grounded in the results. But it over-sells the run in two load-bearing ways that a process-education memo cannot afford: (1) the centerpiece 'false positive' story misrepresents the mechanism — the per-kind verbs were seeded by the workflow's own prompt (script lines 56-57), the skeptics were CORRECT that they're absent from code, and the 'settled paddock vocabulary' rescue is asserted without evidence; and (2) the entire cost argument rests on three precise numbers (~937K tokens, ~200 calls, ~8 min) that appear in NEITHER provided artifact. The single most important change: rewrite the verb/false-positive section to admit the verbs came from the prompt and the skeptics were right about the code, then add an honest scorecard (zero recommendations reversed, one over-confident fact flipped) so the reader sees what verification actually bought on this run — confidence, not error-rescue. Secondary musts: source or hedge the cost figures, and fill the genuine process gaps the prompt asked about (how synthesis actually worked — the workflow does none; why confidence levels mattered — and that high-confidence claims were the wrong ones; why schemas enable retries; the four-calls-to-five-areas re-cut).

result.reviewer: fact-checker (process memo vs. workflow script + result artifact)

issues.where: What it cost: "Ten agents, ~937,000 subagent tokens, ~200 tool calls, about eight minutes of wall-clock."
issues.problem: None of the three cost figures (937K tokens, ~200 tool calls, ~8 min wall-clock) appear anywhere in the result artifact. The artifact's only quantitative scale field is agentCount: 10 (which supports the agent count, not the rest). logs is an empty list. The only ~900K-looking number in the file is '912,122', which is a file:line citation fragment ('rbfd:912,1229,1397,1669') in the scaffold verdict text — not a token count. The token/tool/duration claims are unverifiable from the two artifacts provided and may be fabricated or sourced from somewhere not given.
issues.fix: Either cite the actual source for the 937K/200/8min figures (a run-metrics log distinct from this output file) or soften to clearly-estimated language and state where the numbers came from. If they cannot be sourced, remove the specific numbers and keep only 'ten agents,' which the artifact confirms.

issues.where: What the verify stage caught (false positive): "The skeptic flagged them only because the subagents never received the paddock; they could see the code but not the design document."
issues.problem: The stated cause is wrong. The workflow script's SHARED prompt (line 57) DID hand the per-kind verbs to every investigator verbatim: 'tool (verb fetter, rbw-lF), reliquary (conclave, rbw-lC...), wsl (underpin, rbw-lU), podvm (immure, rbw-lI...)'. So the investigator did not 'invent' the verbs from nothing — it lifted them from its own instructions. The real reason the verifier flagged them as fabricated is that verifyPrompt() does NOT include the SHARED block — the verifier received only the investigation's findings and recommendation_input, never the verb vocabulary — and, re-reading only code, correctly found no such verbs in Tools/rbk. The cause is an investigator-vs-verifier prompt asymmetry, not 'the subagents never received the paddock' (the investigators received the verbs; the verifier did not).
issues.fix: Correct the mechanism: the investigators WERE given the verbs in their shared prompt; the verifier was not (the verify prompt omits the shared vocabulary block), so the skeptic re-reading code alone could not find them and called them fabricated. The lesson ('a verifier is only as good as the context it's given') still holds, but the specific story — verbs as the paddock's settled vocabulary withheld from all subagents — is inaccurate. The verbs are stated as settled in the script's own prompt, and the gap is between the two prompt templates.

issues.where: Over-claimed facts: "The skeptic checked the spec and found only two kinds are actually pinned there" (re trust grades).
issues.problem: Directionally correct but the memo earlier frames the investigator as assigning grades 'to all five kinds at high confidence.' The artifact's investigation claim 7 is a TWO-WAY split, not five independent grades: bole+wsl = verified-against-published; tool+reliquary+podvm = recorded-at-acquisition (confidence 'high'). The verifier marked claim 7 holds=false and noted the spec commits only TWO kinds: bole (RBS0:2187 verified) and podvm (RBS0:2188 recorded reserved) — tool, reliquary, AND wsl are unassigned. The memo's 'only two kinds are actually pinned' is accurate (bole + podvm); but 'assigned trust grades to all five kinds' slightly mischaracterizes the investigator's claim, which presented a two-bucket axis.
issues.fix: Tighten: the investigator presented a two-way trust-grade split covering all five kinds at high confidence; the skeptic found the spec pins only bole (verified) and podvm (recorded), leaving tool/reliquary/wsl as the investigator's inference dressed as fact. Optionally name that bole+podvm are the two pinned kinds, since 'two kinds' otherwise invites the reader to guess wrong.

issues.where: byte-equivalent overstatement: "the full locator differs (`rbi_es/<fp>` vs `rbi_ld/<stamp>:<fp>`)"
issues.problem: The enshrine locator is slightly misquoted. The cutover verdict states enshrine writes 'rbi_es/<fp>:<fp>' (package rbi_es/<fp>, tag <fp>; rbgje01:65), not 'rbi_es/<fp>'. The memo drops the ':<fp>' tag from the enshrine side while keeping it on the Lode side, which understates that the skeptic's point was about package-vs-tag structure differing on both. Substance (only the fingerprint token is byte-identical; full locator differs) is correct.
issues.fix: Quote the artifact's pair exactly: 'rbi_es/<fp>:<fp>' vs 'rbi_ld/<stamp>:<fp>' — same '<fp>' fingerprint token, different package root and package-vs-tag structure.

issues.where: Design choice 1: "the divine/augur read verbs, the per-kind capture bodies, the cutover chokepoints, the provenance/docs surface"
issues.problem: This enumerates only four areas while the run had five investigators/specs (augur, scaffold, vertical, cutover, provdocs). The 'scaffold residue' area (the kind-registration surface ₣BX did not build — its own spec, key 'scaffold') is omitted from this list, though the ASCII diagram above does include 'scaffold'. Minor internal inconsistency: prose says four areas mapped to four calls, but five distinct investigator specs ran.
issues.fix: Either add 'the scaffold/kind-registration residue' to the enumerated list so it names all five areas, or note explicitly that the four open calls were decomposed into five investigator areas (provdocs bundles two questions; scaffold is a fifth area).

issues.where: Closing note: "This memo was itself put through a small ultracode review panel ... checking it for factual accuracy against the workflow's own script and results"
issues.problem: This claim is about a separate review run and is neither supported nor contradicted by the two artifacts in scope (the script and the wl3aagbey output). It is unverifiable from the provided evidence — flagging only so it is not read as confirmed by the artifacts. (If the panel did fact-check against the script, the two high-severity errors above suggest it missed them.)
issues.fix: No change required for accuracy against these artifacts; just be aware this self-referential claim is outside what the script/output can corroborate. If the review panel ran, the cost figures and the false-positive-cause error indicate gaps it did not catch.

result.verdict: Mostly accurate on structure but NOT shippable as-is: two high-severity problems. (1) The cost figures (~937K tokens, ~200 tool calls, ~8 min) are absent from the result artifact — it carries only agentCount: 10 and an empty logs list; the lone ~900K number is a 'rbfd:912,...' file:line citation, not tokens. Source or remove them. (2) The memo's explanation of the per-kind-verb false positive is wrong: the verbs were handed to every investigator in the script's SHARED prompt (line 57), and the verifier flagged them as fabricated because verifyPrompt omits that shared vocabulary, not because 'the subagents never received the paddock.' Everything else checks out and is well-supported: 10 agents (5 investigate + 5 verify via pipeline, not barrier), the claim/evidence/confidence + recommendation_input schema, and the four caught items (byte-equivalent overstatement with the rbi_es/rbi_ld locator split, over-assigned trust grades pinned to only bole+podvm, citation slips in augur/vertical/provdocs, and the verb false positive). Single most important fix: correct or source the cost figures, and fix the false-positive root-cause story.
